title: Stealing Deposits: Deposit Insurance, Risk-Taking, and the Removal of Market Discipline in Early 20th-Century Banks
abstract: Deposit insurance reduces liquidity risk but can increase insolvency risk by encouraging reckless behavior. Several U.S. states installed deposit insurance laws before the creation of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, and those laws applied only to some depository institutions within those states. These experiments present a unique testing ground for investigating the effect of deposit insurance. We show that deposit insurance removed market discipline constraining uninsured banks. Taking advantage of World War I's rise in world agricultural prices, insured banks increased their insolvency risk and competed aggressively for deposits. When prices fell after the war, the insurance systems collapsed and suffered high losses.

article.introduction:
Deposit insurance spread throughoutthe world in the latter half of the 20thcentury as a result of external and internal political pressures favoring its adoption (Demirgüç-Kunt, Kane, and Laeven (2008)). The International Monetary Fund, the European Union, and the World Bank have all endorsed deposit insurance. Despite its overwhelming political support, however, a large empirical literature suggests that the moral-hazard costs of deposit insurance have outweighed its liquidity-risk-reduction benefits.1These papers show that deposit insurance is among the most important contributors to the unprecedented wave of banking crises that has washed over the world during the past four decades. The separation between policy recommendations and economic studies begs the question of whether empirical studies have failed to properly control for the other influences that have contributed to both the rise of deposit insurance and banking instability.
Most studies of deposit insurance are based on cross-country comparisons or comparisons over time within countries that contrast the behavior of insured banking systems with uninsured banking systems.2Despite attempts by authors to control for factors that coincide with the creation or expansion of deposit insurance through explicit controls or through instruments that explain the creation of deposit insurance, it is conceivable that some of the positive association between deposit insurance and increased bank risk may reflect exogenous increases in risk that encourage the passage of deposit insurance. If true, then the risk-creating effects of deposit insurance would be exaggerated.
In this study, we examine a near-ideal environment from the standpoint of identification—the state deposit insurance experiments of the early 20thcentury in the United States.3These systems installed deposit insurance for unit state-chartered commercial banks that operated in parallel to the uninsured system of national banks (i.e., unit banks that were chartered by the Comptroller of the Currency) within a given state and in parallel to uninsured state and national banks operating in bordering nondeposit insurance states. Mitigating the omitted variable problem embodied in cross-country studies, this period allows us to study insured and uninsured depository institutions operating at the same time and in the same place under the same legal system and currency. Using detailed information about banks’ locations, economic environments, and balance sheet characteristics, we implement a difference-in-difference-in-difference model that measures the effect of deposit insurance on insured banks controlling for both the change in uninsured banks in deposit insurance states and the change in uninsured banks in other states. Moreover, because several of the laws were passed in the same year but implemented in different subsequent years, we are able to use placebo tests to determine whether a region-specific economic shock was responsible for changes in banks’ and depositors’ behavior in addition to the passage of deposit insurance, or whether changes in behavior were the consequence of deposit insurance.
Our findings not only corroborate prior literature on the moral-hazard consequences of deposit insurance, but also showhowthe introduction of deposit insurance created systemic risk. We find strong evidence that deposit insurance caused risk to increase in the banking system by removing the market discipline that had been constraining uninsured banks’ decision making. Depositors applied strict market discipline on uninsured banks when evaluating whether to place deposits in those banks, but put relatively little weight on the financial soundness of insured banks. Insured banks were thus able to use the promise of insurance to compete away deposits from uninsured banks. Because they were constrained only by regulatory standards (i.e., a minimum capital-to-deposits ratio, a minimum reserves-to-deposit ratio, and in some cases, a maximum interest rate paid on deposits), which often proved inadequate to prevent insolvency, insured banks raised their loans, reduced their cash reserves, and kept their capital ratios close to the regulatory minimum.
Insured banks seemingly bet on the permanence of the agricultural price increases that occurred during World War I (WWI), and depositors seemingly believed in the insurance systems’ ability to protect them. Deposits flowed most strongly into insured banks located in counties where the price rises had the biggest effect. As insured banks most often used those deposits to fund new loans, the implementation of deposit insurance allowed an asset price bubble in farm land to quickly form. When prices reversed in the early 1920s, the insured banking systems collapsed, leaving depositors with losses.
The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIreviews the details of the early 20th-century state-deposit insurance systems and summarizes the aggregate data on the changing allocation of deposits that accompanied the passage of deposit insurance. SectionIIdescribes our data set. SectionIIIreports our findings on the effects of deposit insurance on insured and uninsured banks’ deposits, loans, cash-to-assets ratio, and leverage. SectionIVexamines whether deposit insurance led to the relaxation of market discipline and changed the nature of competition in the deposit market. SectionVdiscusses the collapses of the insured banking systems during the 1920s. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. State Deposit Insurance Schemes of th:
The United States was a bank liability insurance innovator, installing the only deposit insurance systems for nearly 150 years. The unique propensity for liability insurance in the United States reflected political influences that also produced the nation's unique single-office (“unit banking”) system (for a review of the history of liability insurance, see SectionIof the Internet Appendix, which may be found in the online version of this article at the publisher's website). While several states installed liability insurance funds during the antebellum period, none lasted beyond the Civil War and it was not until the early 1900s that another wave of laws passed. During that wave, eight states passed deposit insurance laws from 1907 to 1917. Each law created a nonstate-guaranteed fund that would be used to reimburse any deposits in the event of a failed insured bank. The laws stipulated each bank's annual assessment (typically a very small fraction of its deposits minus capital) as well as the maximum extra assessment that each bank could be forced to pay to replenish the fund during an emergency. While the laws typically included national banks in their systems, the Comptroller of the Currency prohibited national banks from joining, and the Supreme Court upheld that prohibition.
The major differences across the deposit insurance systems revolved around whether insurance was mandatory. Kansas and Washington passed voluntary laws that gave state banks discretion as to whether to opt into the system. Texas allowed banks to opt out of the state's deposit insurance system if they insured their deposits by posting a collateral bond. South Dakota also initially passed a voluntary deposit insurance law, but that did not give rise to an insurance system because the creation of the system did not reach the required number of members specified by the law. When given a choice, many banks chose not to join the system. About 40% of state banks in Kansas, 65% of state banks in Washington, and 10% of state banks in Texas opted out of insurance while retaining their state charters (Wheelock and Kumbhakar (1995), State Bank Examiner of Washington (1918–1920), Hooks and Robinson (2002)).
The laws were geographically concentrated. Figure1shows the geographical pattern of insured states and the comparison group of noninsured neighboring states that we will focus on in our analysis. Oklahoma (whose law took effect in 1908), Texas and Kansas (which took effect in 1909), Nebraska (whose law passed a law in 1909 but was not active until a second law was passed in 1911), South Dakota (whose law passed a law in 1909 but was not active until a second law was passed in 1916), and North Dakota (which took effect in 1918) form a column down the middle of the country. The two geographic outliers (Mississippi in 1914 and Washington in 1917) appear to have installed their systems as a hasty reaction to bank failures in their state (Robb (1921)).
Small and undiversified banks in rural areas were the main supporters of deposit insurance as they had the most to gain from protection against credit and liquidity risk. Large state banks and national banks in urban areas fought the legislation as they did not want to be responsible for risky agricultural loans. Therefore, as highlighted in TableI, insurance laws were passed in states where small state banks were more plentiful.4White (1981, p. 539) explains that deposit insurance states had “firmly established unit banking within their boundaries and were all in relatively undiversified regions where business prosperity in general depended on one or two commodities.” White's empirical analysis shows that deposit insurance was passed in states with low minimum capital requirements for state banks (or alternatively, low average state bank size) and unit banking. The agricultural states in the Midwest and South Central United States fit this general pattern and were prime candidates for deposit insurance. Indeed, many other states in these regions (e.g., Colorado, Minnesota, and Missouri) narrowly defeated deposit insurance legislation during the period, yet few states outside of these regions considered or voted upon deposit insurance (Cooke (1910), White (1981)).
To examine the effects of the legislation, Figure2illustrates aggregate deposits separately for early deposit insurance adopters (i.e., Oklahoma, Texas, Kansas, and Nebraska), late deposit insurance adopters (i.e., Mississippi, South Dakota, North Dakota, and Washington), and nonadopters of deposit insurance in the geographic comparison group. The figure shows that deposit insurance states experienced similar unconditional growth trends as nondeposit insurance states around the dates insurance was installed. From 1908 through 1914, deposits grew by 39% in the comparison states and by 42% in the early adopter states. The only large differential growth between deposit insurance and nondeposit insurance states occurred after 1914. From 1914 through 1919, deposits grew by 90% in the comparison states but by 139% in the early adopters. This differential growth coincided with the large increase in crop prices during WWI. Figure3shows that prices more than doubled from 1914 to 1919 under the influence of WWI's contraction in global supply, and fell nearly as quickly after the war was over. As Internet Appendix TableIA.IIshows, farm real estate values also substantially rose and then declined in agricultural states, particularly Western states (Alston, Grove, and Wheelock (1994), Rajan and Ramcharan (2015)).
Figure4displays the ratio of state bank deposits to national bank deposits (normalized by values in 1900). There are clear jumps in state banking relative to national banking surrounding the passage of deposit insurance. State bank deposits jump sharply relative to national bank deposits in 1908 and 1909 for early adopters and display a smaller rise in 1916 and 1917 for late adopters. Between 1907 and 1912, the deposit ratio grew by 2% for the comparison states, –10% for late adopters, and 31% for early adopters. Between 1913 and 1919, the corresponding numbers are 9%, 22%, and 10%. The jumps correspond to the dates of deposit insurance's passage and thus are not the result of a broader trend. The ratios also clearly illustrate that the boom in agricultural areas during WWI led to the expansion of state bank deposits in all states, but more so in deposit insurance states.
The aggregate data allow us to perform a placebo test to examine whether the factors that led to a law's passage also led to the expansion of state bank deposits, or whether it was the actual use of the insurance law that mattered. Nebraska and South Dakota passed deposit insurance laws in 1909 that were stillborn. It was not until additional laws were passed in Nebraska in 1911 and in South Dakota in 1915 (and implemented in 1916) that deposit insurance became a reality in those states. If the laws and the bank changes were both the result of some omitted state-level factor, then we would expect state bank growth to occur in 1909 in both states. However, if the growth was the result of the implementation of deposit insurance itself, then we should see the growth occur in 1911 for Nebraska and in 1916 for South Dakota. Figure5shows a slight growth in state bank deposits relative to national bank deposits after the passage of the inactive law in South Dakota, but the same pattern is also visible for the nondeposit insurance comparison states (growing 10% between 1909 and 1910) in Figure4. Thus, there is not a large jump in state bank deposits in the two deposit insurance states compared to nondeposit insurance comparison states until after the deposit insurance funds were made active. We conclude that the anticipation of insurance was not enough to spur depositors to move their deposits. We also conclude from the figures that the differential rise in state banks' deposits after deposit insurance was not a function of differential exogenous influences on growth.
article.II. Data:
We construct two databases to investigate deposit competition and risk attributes of banks before and after enacting deposit insurance. For each database, we restrict the sample to six states that adopted deposit insurance and the nondeposit insurance states adjacent to them. We do not include Mississippi and Washington for three reasons: (i) they did not provide bank-level information for the entire period, (ii) they are geographic outliers that do not fit well with our comparison group of states, and (iii) there is evidence that they installed deposit insurance as a result of an idiosyncratic negative banking shock.
The first database contains annual state-level data that cover all banks and states from 1900 through 1930. The data come from All Bank Statistics and were digitized by Flood (1998). These data provide a separate breakdown of the aggregate balance sheet of all state banks and all national banks in each state. Thus, for each variable of interest and each year, there are two observations per state, one for state banks and one for national banks.
The second database contains biennial bank-level data from 1900 through 1920. National bank data were published by the Comptroller of the Currency, and state bank data were published separately by each state. While the national bank data that were digitized by Jaremski (2013) are complete for each year, many states did not publish state bank data until after 1907 and most only published information every other year (Mitchener and Jaremski (2015)). We digitized the data of all states in the sample region that published reports from 1900 through 1920.5As shown in TableII, missing states are usually in the west (e.g., Arizona and Utah), where banking was still in its infancy. When single-year gaps exist in the data for a particular state, we interpolate those state bank values.6Our method begins with a linear interpolation of each individual state bank's observations, which is the midpoint of the two surrounding years. We then adjust the interpolated values by uniformly shifting all of the values so that the average change in the imputed values is identical to the observed change that year in the aggregate value of that variable for state banks in the state. The resulting biennial database contains 65,730 observations and spans 9,027 state banks and 1,925 national banks.7Summary statistics for the main variables of interest are provided in Internet Appendix TableIA.III.
We construct our data set to facilitate comparisons of bank behavior across bank charter types and between states that enact deposit insurance and their neighboring states.8This design permits controlled comparisons between otherwise similar banks operating in similar economic environments (uninsured and insured banks within the same states, or insured banks and uninsured banks in neighboring states) and avoids less relevant comparisons (say, between the growth of banks in a Western agricultural state like Nebraska and those in Rhode Island or Maine). For that reason, our sample does not include banks in states that never bordered a deposit insurance state.9
Historical banking reports did not report income statement information. Nor did they collect information about dividend payments or the interest rates paid on deposits (as discussed more fully in the Internet Appendix). Therefore, data on interest and profit rates are unavailable for the period at the bank or state level, and hence we cannot calculate profit rates or control for the interest rate provided to depositors.
We augment the bank balance sheet data in several ways. We employ county-level Census data assembled by Haines (2004), which contain information for each decade. Aggregating counties to their 1900 boundaries, we assume that the census variables grew linearly over time, which permits us to construct annual estimates from the decennial observations. These controls are particularly important due to the agricultural expansion during WWI. We also add information on whether a county had a clearinghouse from Jaremski (2015).
article.III. The Influence of Deposit Insurance:
In this section, we test these hypotheses to examine whether (i) insured banks increased their share of deposits relative to uninsured banks as a consequence of receiving deposit insurance, (ii) whether they used those deposits to fund loans, and (iii) whether they targeted a higher level of default risk by maintaining lower cash-to-assets and capital-to-assets ratios.
Several identification concerns confront studies of the consequences of enacting deposit insurance for bank behavior. These concerns raise questions about whether the observed changes in behavior that coincide with the enactment of insurance reflect causal influences of deposit insurance or other concurrent factors.
First, deposit insurance states could be structurally different from nondeposit insurance states, and might have had persistently higher deposit growth or risk-taking than other states. Second, unobserved idiosyncratic shocks may have occurred only in deposit insurance states around the dates that they enacted deposit insurance. The placebo tests we report in Figure5as well as the wide range of deposit insurance passage dates over time argue against this concern, but it is still conceivable that observed changes in behavior by insured banks may reflect factors specific to the banking system of those states around that time other than deposit insurance. Third, all state banks could have been growing differentially relative to national banks during the period when deposit insurance was installed. The differences between state and national banks in deposit insurance states after the enactment of deposit insurance may therefore simply reflect a continuation of pre-existing differences in trends or even a region-wide structural break in the difference between state banks and national banks that happened around the same time as deposit insurance.
In the empirical analysis reported here, we take advantage of the unique structure of deposit insurance laws and the locations of banks operating under different charters to overcome the three identification challenges listed above and identify the causal effect of deposit insurance on bank outcomes. First, we show that neither deposit insurance states nor state banks within those states had differential trends in either deposits or loans prior to the passage of the first deposit insurance law in 1908. Second, in addition to various economic and demographic controls as well as fixed effects, we include both uninsured national banks and uninsured state banks as control groups. National banks (which operated in each state under uniform chartering rules) were present in the same locations as insured state banks yet could not join the insurance systems. They therefore offer a within-state comparison group to capture shocks that would have influenced all banks in the state, not just insured ones.10The inclusion of uninsured state-chartered banks in the model then provides an across-state comparison group that controls for opportunities and risks that were specific to state-chartered banks. Finally, we show that results are robust to various potential concerns by analyzing each of the insurance systems one at a time against a comparison group of neighboring banks.
Putting the model into the context of identification concerns, the bank fixed effects control for the possibility that deposit insurance states and state banks in those states were always different. The time fixed effects capture changes in each year that were common to all banks and thus control for macroeconomic factors and changes in federal regulation. AndDIs,taccounts for the effect of the introduction of deposit insurance on all banks in a state relative to banks in nondeposit insurance states.
The interaction betweenStateBankiandDIAreas,taccounts for the possibility that all state banks in the region (insured and uninsured) may have started to grow differently from national banks around the same time that deposit insurance was installed in their area.15Because this interaction variable takes a value of 1 only after the state or one nearby installed deposit insurance, we can be confident that the variable is picking up any local shocks that might lead to differential state bank behavior that would otherwise be associated falsely with deposit insurance. Before 1915, the interaction variable is identified by comparing state banks near deposit insurance states with national banks in all states and with state banks not near deposit insurance states. After 1915, the interaction variable reflects only a comparison of state banks with national banks. Its inclusion decreases the estimated deposit insurance effect toward zero because state banks’ regulations better positioned them to fund the agricultural boom created by WWI.
Finally, the coefficient on the interaction betweenDIs,tandInsuredi(β3), when controlling for all other influences, is a conservative measure of the causal effect of deposit insurance on insured state banks. When all controls are included, β3measures the covariance of deposit insurance with bank behavior for state banks in deposit insurance states after controlling for differential growth of all banks in deposit insurance states (β1) and differential state bank growth relative to national banks in nondeposit insurance states (β2). Note that the estimated growth of national banks (as well as uninsured state banks in Kansas) in deposit insurance states is captured by β1, whereas the estimated growth of insured banks in deposit insurance states is reflected by the sum,β1+β2+β3.
Because uninsured banks were negatively affected by deposit insurance (through the “stealing” of deposits), β1reflects a combination of two factors: local economic influences that affected all banks, and competition for deposits from insured banks. Under the null hypothesis that deposit insurance has no effect in promoting “stealing” of deposits from uninsured banks, β1only reflects the first factor, and β3is an unbiased measure of the influence of deposit insurance. Alternatively, if deposit insurance promotes growth in insured banks in part by taking deposits from uninsured banks, then β3is a biased estimate of the total effect of deposit insurance by the degree of “stealing.”16The estimated coefficients, however, show that even when β1is assumed to be entirely the result of “stealing,” rather than capturing local economic influences affecting all banks, the treatment effect of insurance on the deposits (or loans) of insured banks (β1+β3)is still large, positive, and statistically significant.
To place an upper bound on the proportion of β1that reflects “stealing” rather than economic circumstances, we ran a separate set of regressions (similar to those in TablesVandVI) in which we include in the sample only national banks in insurance and noninsurance states. A comparison between those results (reported in Internet Appendix TableIA.IV) and the results reported in TablesVandVIallows us to examine the extent to which changes in national bank outcomes after the installation of deposit insurance in deposit insurance states differed from national bank outcomes in nondeposit insurance states. As national banks were subject to the same regulatory changes across states and were subject to common regional economic opportunities, the β1coefficient onDIin the states with deposit insurance captures only the competitive effect of the presence of insured banks on national banks (i.e., “stealing”). In other words, under the assumption of regional environmental influences common to all national banks in the sample region, we can compare the coefficients of the national bank-specific regressions in TableIA.IVwith the coefficients of the full bank sample in TablesVandVIto provide an upper bound estimate on the proportion of β1that is due to “stealing.”
That comparison indicates that the “stealing” effect from local national banks represents between a third and three-fourths of the β1coefficients, depending on the dependent variable being considered. Specifically, the “stealing” effect represents 72.5% of the β1coefficient for deposits, 47.7% for loans, 31.0% for cash-to-assets, and 61.9% for capital-to-assets. We conclude that while the “stealing effect” influences β1, such influence does not qualitatively affect our conclusions regarding the consequences of deposit insurance.
TableIIIreports estimates of equation2. We find that banks in deposit insurance states did not display different trends before 1908 compared to banks in noninsurance states (i.e., β1is not significantly different from zero), and that state banks in deposit insurance states did not have different trends from national banks in those states before 1908 (i.e., β2is not significantly different from zero).
Next, we examine aggregate bank behavior at the state level after the passage of deposit insurance. While bank-level data allow us to control better for idiosyncratic differences, the state-level data permit us to analyze the overall effects of deposit insurance on the relative sizes of insured and uninsured banking systems as a whole, measured by deposits, loans, and the number of banks. The aggregate state-level results are presented in TableIV. The pattern of coefficients in columns (1) and (3) imply no significant effect of deposit insurance on the number of banks operating in insured states or the total amount of deposits, but the coefficient in column (5) implies that total lending increased substantially in the presence of insurance in a state. When the interactions are included (in columns (2), (4), and (6)), deposit insurance of state banks is associated with an increase in the value of both aggregate deposits and aggregate loans for state banks relative to national banks. The coefficient on the interaction betweenDIs,tandStateBankiis positive, large, and statistically significant for both deposits and loans. The coefficient onDIs,tis negative but statistically insignificant, suggesting that some but not all national banks lost deposits and loans due to deposit insurance. As discussed in SectionV, the national bank aggregates are influenced by several large state banks that switched to a national bank charter rather than join the insurance system.
We can use the estimated coefficients in TableIVto measure the magnitude of the effects of insurance. Between the installation of deposit insurance and 1920, national bank aggregates in a nondeposit insurance state lost 5.4% in deposits (column (4)), state bank aggregates in a nondeposit insurance state gained 10.2%, and state bank deposit aggregates in a deposit insurance state gained 30% (i.e., –0.054 + 0.102 + 0.252). For loans in column (6), the comparable numbers are –5.4% for national banks, 9.2% for uninsured state banks, and 35.8% for insured state banks (i.e., –0.054 + 0.092 + 0.320). These results support Hypothesis 1.
We can express these numbers in dollar values using the state-level averages for national banks ($75 million for deposits and $58 million for loans) and for state banks ($64 million for deposits and $56 million for loans) in 1908. The estimated losses to national bank aggregates were $4 million in deposits and $3 million in loans whereas the estimated gains to state bank aggregates were $19 million in deposits and $20 million in loans. Thus, even though national banks were larger, their percentage losses were still relatively small compared to the percentage gains at state banks. These estimates show that the “stealing” of preexisting deposits explains only a small fraction of the increase in insured banks’ deposits and loans, leaving a larger portion to be explained by insured banks’ ability to attract new depositors.
The coefficients onStateBank*DIAreaindicate that state banks grew larger in all states around the passage of deposit insurance, not just those that were insured. While the result is not highly statistically significant for deposits, it is large and economically significant. State banks, with their more lenient regulatory requirements, were probably best equipped to fund the WWI agricultural boom. The inclusion of the state bank interaction is an important control that conservatively biases the estimated effects of deposit insurance toward zero.
Employing individual bank-level data substantially improves the causal identification of deposit insurance's effects. Not only is the sample size much larger, but we are able to control for fixed characteristics of individual banks and better account for local (county-level) characteristics that influence deposit growth at particular banks.18Banks operated from a particular location under “unit” (single-office) banking restrictions, so we match each bank with its county's demographic and economic characteristics in place of state aggregates, and we employ additional control variables for local bank competition (using the numbers of state and national banks in the county) and local financial development (using a dummy variable denoting whether the county had a clearinghouse association in operation). Moreover, we are able to separate the state banks in Kansas that opted out of the voluntary system from insured banks and use them as an additional comparison group.
The bank-level results reported in TableVare similar to the state-level results. In the bank-level results, there are stronger negative effects for national banks and slightly weaker positive effects for state banks than the aggregate results reported in TableIV. On average, a national bank in a deposit insurance state, ceteris paribus, is predicted to have lost 13.8% of its deposits (column (1)) and 6.5% of its loans (column (5)) as a result of the introduction of deposit insurance, whereas an insured state bank is predicted to have gained 30.8% more deposits and 24.9% more loans.
As before, it is useful to think of the total estimated change in deposits and loans for each group of banks. On average, after the installation of deposit insurance, the model estimates that national banks in deposit insurance states lost 13.8% of deposits, uninsured state banks in non deposit insurance states lost 1.4% of deposits, and insured state banks gained 15.6% (i.e., –0.138 –0.014 + 0.308). The effects on loans are larger, with national banks losing 6.5%, uninsured state banks gaining 6.8%, and insured state banks gaining 25.2% (i.e., −0.065 + 0.068 + 0.249). Taking the average values for individual state and national banks (see Internet Appendix TableIA.III), these percentages imply a loss of $23,248 in deposits and $10,356 in loans for each national bank and a gain of $10,559 in deposits and $15,226 in loans for each insured state bank. Because the average ratio of state banks to national banks was just under 3-to-1 in the region (see TableI), the aggregate effect is a small positive gain for deposits and a larger positive gain for loans. Thus, when controlling for local as well as regional economic growth, we find support for Hypothesis 1: insured banks increased their share of deposits relative to uninsured banks. Moreover, the growth in deposits seems to have permitted insured banks to expand their loans.
To what extent did the gains of deposits and loans for insured banks vary with bank characteristics?19Deposit insurance legislation was advocated by small banks that believed they stood to gain most from its protection (White (1983), Calomiris and White (1994)). In Internet Appendix TableIA.XI, we interactDI * Insuredwith a dummy denoting whether the bank was in the lower quartile of capital for all banks.20We also interactDI * Insuredwith a dummy denoting whether the bank appeared in the sample after 1903 to capture whether deposit insurance was particularly helpful in attracting deposits for new banks that lacked a reputation. The results in columns (2), (3), (6), and (7) of TableVshow that small and young state banks benefited the most from deposit insurance legislation. A small insured state bank is estimated to have gained 17.3% more deposits and 21.2% more loans than a large insured state bank, whereas a young insured state bank gained an additional 6.1% more deposits and 6.3% more loans than an old insured state bank. As columns (3) and (7) show, when assessing the effect of bank size, it is important to control for the fact that young banks of all kinds tend to have higher growth rates. Young national banks in insured states saw 29.5% higher deposit growth than older national banks.
Columns (4) and (8) of TableVexamine whether the effects of deposit insurance on deposits and loans depended on the local economic environment. In particular, the ability of banks to grow their loans and their risk may have depended on the existence of risk-taking opportunities. The historical narrative of these state deposit insurance systems has emphasized that insured banks funded loans to expand cultivation of crops experiencing a WWI-induced priced boom. The local crop mix therefore implied different risk opportunities across locations. In areas with crops that were experiencing the WWI-induced price increase, there was ample opportunity to take risk because the expansion of cultivation was risky, given the likely relative decline in prices that would follow the end of the war (which, when it did occur, proved disastrous for banks involved in agricultural lending to affected farmers).
To generate a realistic measure of the local effect of WWI on banks, we measure the change in local crop value at the county level. We first construct a measure of local crop mix based on crop production numbers for each crop in 1910 using Haines (2004). We then create an annual measure of crop value per capita at the county level (Crop Value) by applying the price of each crop each year in aggregate (from Carter et al. (2006)) to the county-level crop mix. Note that the variable captures only thecross-sectionaldifferences among counties in their risk environment each year as the year fixed effects remove the average effect of the WWI price run-up.
Columns (4) and (8) of TableVshow that the local risk environment mattered for the deposit and loan growth of insured banks. Consistent with the historical narrative, insured banks were riding the crop price bubble of WWI. The interactions are positive and significant for both loans and deposits, and account for a large proportion of the overall effect of deposit insurance. Comparing columns (1) and (4), the coefficient onDI * Insuredfalls from 0.308 with ap-value under 0.001 to only 0.109 with ap-value of 0.210. In other words, most of the effect of deposit insurance on loan and deposit growth can be traced to the behavior of insured banks in counties that were experiencing an agricultural price boom.
When we limit the sample to the pre-WWI period (see TableIA.XII), we find that the average effect of deposit insurance on deposits and loans is 0.204 and 0.157, respectively, compared to 0.308 and 0.249 in TableV. WhenCrop Valueis included in these regressions, the coefficients onDI * Insuredfor deposits and loans are roughly half the size of those reported in TableVand are no longer statistically significant. These results confirm the previous findings that much, but not all, of the influence of deposit insurance on deposit and loan growth reflected responses to the WWI price run up.
The bank-level results indicate that insured banks attracted more depositors than other banks (Hypothesis 1), but a question that remains is whether they also took on more failure risk (Hypothesis 2). The growth of loans is suggestive of greater risk, but is not conclusive. Bank failure risk depends on the (unobserved) riskiness of borrowers, the composition of the asset portfolio (i.e., the cash-to-assets ratio), and bank leverage (i.e., the capital-to-assets ratio). We therefore examine how banks’ cash-to-assets and capital-to-assets ratios changed once they became covered by insurance. Higher cash-to-assets reduces insolvency risk by reducing the riskiness of assets and the illiquidity risk of the bank. A higher capital ratio reduces insolvency risk by increasing the capital buffer of junior equity claims (relative to bank debt) that can absorb losses.
The results in TableVIconfirm the view that the installation of deposit insurance encouraged insured banks to take more risk. Insured state banks increased their leverage (i.e., lowered their capital-to-assets ratio) and reduced the proportion of cash holdings in assets. Specifically, an insured bank is found to decrease its cash-to-assets ratio by 3.5% and its capital-to-assets ratio by 2.6%. At the same time, the effect on national banks is positive in both cases. The typical national bank operating in a state with deposit insurance increased its cash-to-assets ratio by 4.2% and its capital ratio by 2.1%. This pattern may reflect rising riskiness of lending during the period: consistent with our theoretical model described in SectionIIof the Internet Appendix, national banks targeting a constant level of default risk would have had to respond to rising loan risk by increasing their proportion of cash to assets and reducing leverage.
Young and small insured banks especially decreased their cash and capital ratios. A young insured state bank decreased its cash-to-assets ratio by an additional 1.3% and its capital-to-assets ratio by an additional 1.6% after controlling for growth in national banks in its own state and the growth in uninsured state banks in other states.
As in TableV, insured state banks in counties with large changes in crop value saw the biggest decreases in cash and capital ratios. In columns (4) and (8) of TableVI, the presence of the additional interaction of deposit insurance andCrop Valuereduces the two ratios so much that it accounts for the entirety of the effect of deposit insurance. The inclusion of this interaction in column (4) even causes the simple average effect of deposit insurance on the cash ratio to flip in sign, indicating that theCrop Valueinteractions account for 200% of the average effect.
Clearly, insured banks were expanding their risk as part of the risk-taking process of the agricultural boom in crop prices. As suggested by Rajan and Ramcharan (2015), deposit insurance likely helped magnify the investment bubble that developed in the late 1910s. This result is significant in light of the subsequent agricultural price collapse, and the disruption to the banking system and the local economies that it produced, which we discuss in SectionV.
When we limit the sample to the pre-WWI period (see Internet Appendix TableIA.XIII), we find that the average effects of deposit insurance on the cash-to-assets ratio and the capital-to-assets ratio are smaller than those in TableVI, but remain statistically significant. These results confirm the findings in TableVIthat much, but not all, of the influence of deposit insurance on deposit and loan growth reflects responses to the WWI price rise.
The conclusions that we draw from the difference-in difference-in-difference regressions based on the full sample of banks from the comparison region (consisting of national banks, insured state banks, and uninsured state banks) rely on comparability of the banks in the sample. Although we believe that this assumption is reasonable given the proximity of these locations to one another, their similar dependence on agriculture, and the use of county-level controls and bank and year fixed effects in our estimation, we also consider a narrower sample on which to base our comparisons. In results reported in Internet Appendix TablesIA.XVandIA.XVI, we include only banks located in counties along the border between a deposit insurance state and a nondeposit insurance state. These banks are located within a few hundred miles of each other and should experience the same economic or environmental conditions. This sample allows us to gauge whether the results in the full sample are driven by locational differences. As the coefficients onDI * Insuredare not statistically significantly different from those in TablesVandVI, we conclude that banks throughout the comparison states operated in similar economic environments after including the various control variables.21
Previous studies of these state deposit insurance experiments noted that the voluntary systems experienced smaller bank failure rates, and argued that the risk-promoting effects of deposit insurance were mitigated in voluntary systems (Calomiris (1989,1990,1992)). When deposit insurance is voluntary, large, low-risk state-chartered banks can opt out of the system without converting to a national bank charter and facing its more stringent requirements. Thus, in voluntary deposit insurance systems, where cross-subsidization of risk within the system is limited by the greater ability of large established banks to opt out of the insurance system, the risk-promoting effects of deposit insurance may be mitigated.
Even if this logic is true for banking systems as a whole, it is not obvious whether individual insured banks would take more or less risk in a voluntary system. On the one hand, less cross-subsidization of risk might make depositors discipline risk-taking by insured banks more, thus constraining their risks. On the other hand, if voluntary insurance selects riskier banks into the system and depositor discipline is not strong, then individual bank risk-taking may be greater, even though its systemic consequences are more limited. We therefore regard the theoretical predictions of voluntary insurance's effect on individual bank risk-taking as ambiguous.
To investigate differences in individual bank risk-taking associated with voluntary insurance, we divide the sample into three groups based on the three deposit insurance systems in our sample and their closest uninsured neighbor. In the analysis of Kansas's voluntary system, we include only insured and uninsured state banks located in the state.22For the compulsory insurance system of Nebraska, we include state and national banks in Nebraska as well as state and national banks located in Iowa. For the compulsory system of South Dakota, we similarly include state and national banks in South Dakota as well as state and national banks located in Minnesota and Montana.
The results in TableVIIimply that voluntary insurance somewhat mitigated the risk-taking by insured banks for all four endogenous variables. In columns (2) and (3), the absolute value of the coefficient onDI * Insuredfor loans and for the cash-to-assets ratio is lower for Kansas than for either of the coefficients for Nebraska and South Dakota. In columns (1) and (4), the absolute value of the coefficient onDI * Insuredis lower for Kansas than South Dakota, and is slightly higher for Kansas than Nebraska. In the cases in which the coefficient onDI * Insuredfor Kansas is greater in absolute value than the comparable coefficient for Nebraska, they are never statistically significantly different from each other.
In Internet Appendix TableIA.XIX, we aggregate the three analyses reported in TableVIIinto a single specification, where the responses associated with the two mandatory insurance systems (Nebraska and South Dakota) are constrained to be equal. This permits a formal test to see whether, on average, voluntary insurance differs in its consequences from mandatory insurance. The absolute values of the estimated effects of insurance are greater for mandatory insurance for all four dependent variables, and are statistically significant for all but the capital-to-assets ratio.
Over and above the comparison of voluntary and mandatory systems, the evidence within Kansas is powerful because it shows that insured banks of the exact same type and location as uninsured banks were increasing their relative lending and risk-taking.
Our analysis thus far assumes that deposit insurance had its full effects immediately upon enactment. In reality, it may have taken time for banks and their customers to react to the passage of deposit insurance. Because deposit insurance was passed at different dates in the three sample insurance states, we are able to estimate the adjustment process separately from the influence of common time fixed effects.
Internet Appendix TableIA.XXreports the coefficients for equation3for each of the four dependent variables (deposits, loans, cash-to-assets ratio, and capital-to-assets ratio). With respect to the behavior of insured banks, the coefficients onDI * Insuredfor each year after enactment of deposit insurance show that there is a large immediate effect of deposit insurance for every variable. However, the coefficients for all four variables show increasing absolute values over time (although the cash ratio does not change uniformly).
We next push the model a step further to examine the role of the WWI agricultural price boom in more detail. In the presence of the variables controlling for dynamic adjustment, we can better assess the effects of the price boom. First, we examine whether there is an average time effect of WWI on all insured banks. This is captured by the coefficient onDI * Insured * WWI Period. Here,WWI Periodis an indicator variable that takes a value of 1 for 1916 and 1918. Interestingly, for all outcomes but the cash-to-assets ratio, there is no observed average effect of deposit insurance across all insured banks that is specific to the WWI period. That said, the previous specifications suggest that the price increase affected certain areas more than others. We therefore examine this effect by includingDI * Insured * Crop Valuein lieu ofDI * Insured * WWI Period. In three of the four regressions, this variable continues to be large and statistically significant, which echoes the results reported in TablesVandVI. Overall, this confirms the view that the price run-up of WWI magnified the incentive effects of deposit insurance primarily for banks located in counties experiencing a pronounced price increase. Additionally, the war had a greater effect on all banks operating within insured states, whether or not they were insured banks. The significance of the effect highlights the importance of the inclusion of a rich set of controls in the regressions, especially local risk-taking opportunities.
article.IV. Competing for Deposits: Does Deposit:
In this section we model competition in the market for deposits, where the flow of deposits is the key dependent variable of interest. Hypothesis 3 described in more detail in SectionIIof the Internet Appendix states that the installation of deposit insurance created two classes of banks: disciplined (uninsured) and undisciplined (insured). Insured banks that took more risk were not disciplined because many depositors perceived that the insurance system was credible. Insured banks were able to offer high deposit interest rates because of their greater lending risks and commensurately higher loan interest rates, while deposit insurance protection limited their perceived deposit risk. In contrast, uninsured banks were unable to compete for deposits with insured banks because uninsured banks’ low required risk level prevented them from offering high-interest rates on deposits. Uninsured banks competed with one another for deposits based on their ability to demonstrate to the market that their risk was sufficiently low using observable fundamentals, including their leverage, loan-to-assets ratio, and observable loan risk.
We test this model of deposit competition by examining the response of depositors to balance sheet characteristics of insured and uninsured banks that should have mattered for deposit risk. Following our theoretical framework, we model the flow of deposits as reacting to the previous value of the bank's risk characteristics, which include the loan-to-assets ratio, the capital-to-assets ratio, and the bank's exposure to loan risk as measured by real estate owned relative to assets.23Economic theory and several empirical studies show that deposit flows of uninsured banks respond positively to the capital ratio and negatively to both the loan-to-assets ratio and measures of loan portfolio risk (Martinez-Peria and Schmukler (2001), Calomiris and Powell (2001)). Once deposit insurance is active, we expect the coefficients on each of these three variables to be reduced in absolute value only for insured banks.
The first column of TableVIIIpresents the panel results for our model. For illustrative purposes, in columns (2) to (4) we report estimates that include only one of the three risk measures in the model. The sign on the real estate owned ratio is negative in the presence of all three risk measures, indicating that, ceteris paribus, higher loan risk produces deposit outflow. However, the sign on the real estate owned ratio is positive in the absence of the other two variables. This reflects the high covariance between loan risk and the other two risk measures: banks with risky lending portfolios tend to maintain lower loan ratios and higher capital ratios. If not, then uninsured banks with high levels of real estate owned would not have gained deposits.
The results confirm the hypothesis that, in the absence of deposit insurance, depositors disciplined banks. The loan-to-assets and real estate-to-assets ratios negatively predict deposit growth, while the capital-to-assets ratio positively predicts deposit growth. However, once deposit insurance was in place, insured banks’ risk characteristics became much less relevant to their ability to attract deposits. The coefficients on the three risk characteristics interacted with the presence of deposit insurance have opposite signs from the coefficient values that apply to all banks. The summing of the three sets of coefficients for insured banks indicates a near-zero effect of these risk characteristics for insured deposit flows. Only the capital-to-assets ratio remains significantly different from zero. Simply put, insured depositors seem to have disregarded many of the typical warning signs of increasing default risk for insured banks.
The cross-sectional results in TableIXshow that the removal of discipline occurred primarily during the WWI period. This is consistent with our discussion of TablesVandVI, where we find that the incentive effects of deposit insurance were visible primarily in counties experiencing large increases in crop value during WWI. The coefficient on the deposit insurance interaction is only positive and significant for the loan ratio after 1914. The coefficient on the interaction of real estate owned and deposit insurance is positive and significant only for 1916 through 1920. The coefficient on the interaction of deposit insurance and the capital ratio is only significantly negative from 1914 through 1920. The largest coefficients on the interactions appear in 1916, when commodity prices were taking off. In other words, depositors were ignoring important warning signs of insured banks’ rising default risk specifically when those banks were making very risky bets about the persistence of the WWI price increases.
Providing further information on deposit flows, Internet Appendix TableIA.XXIaccounts for differences in the individual state insurance systems by breaking out the discipline effects separately for Kansas, Nebraska, and South Dakota. The interactions of the three risk measures with the presence of involuntary deposit insurance systems are large in absolute value for all three measures during the WWI era. The voluntary system in Kansas displays somewhat weaker effects on the interactions for two of the three risk measures, but not for the capital ratio.
article.V. When the Bubble Collapsed:
Our analysis of the effects of deposit insurance on bank risk-taking shows that insured banks rode the asset price bubble that formed as a result of WWI. What happened once prices collapsed beginning in 1920? It is conceivable that deposit insurance could have helped cushion the state banking system during the downturn by mitigating depositors’ incentives to run on banks. Alternatively, deposit insurance may have exacerbated the collapse because it allowed banks to take on greater risk. Also, once depositors realized the implications of agricultural price collapses for the insurance fund, the resulting collapse of confidence may have hastened depositor runs. Recall that the credibility of deposit insurance could be called into question because the funds were not state-guaranteed. A sufficiently large shock to the system could (and did) result in a failure to cover insured deposits.
The existing literature shows that deposit insurance did not shield the systems from runs and collapses.25All deposit insurance systems suspended during the 1920s, and all but Texas's voluntary system produced depositor losses. Calomiris (1992) finds that insurance states worsened the contraction of deposits during the 1920s and the magnitude of losses to depositors. Wheelock and Wilson (1995) show that insured state banks failed more often than uninsured state banks in Kansas during the downturn, Hooks and Robinson (2002) show a higher failure rate of insured banks in Texas, and Alston, Grove, and Wheelock (1994) show that the agricultural downturn caused more rural bank failures in deposit insurance states. A study of bank behavior during the downturn by Wheelock and Kumbhakar (1994) also shows that insured banks maintained their risky portfolio distributions even in the face of failures. Although Chung and Richardson (2006) do not find a connection between greater bank suspensions and deposit insurance during these banking system collapses, they find an unusually large rise in the number of suspensions caused by mismanagement relative to other states. Studies of economic growth during the period, such as Dehejia and Lleras-Muney (2007), also indicate that deposit insurance's potentially positive state-level effect on loans during WWI was wiped away during the 1920s, and deposit insurance states saw significantly less financial and economic growth.
In the latter part of 1920 and in 1921 came the nationwide wave of bank failures accompanying the deflationary policies of that period. The impact of the deflation on banks in Oklahoma was doubtless accentuated by the laxity of bank supervision after 1919 but the major factor in the situation was the collapse in values, particularly in farm products….With numerous additional failures in 1922, and a sharp contraction of deposits in participating banks and therefore in the income from assessments, there was no hope of restoring the system to solvency.
Similar events played out in the other deposit insurance states. Even a few failures put severe pressure on the already notoriously small insurance funds, and the insured systems lacked the ability to quickly replenish the fund. The systems often issued warrants on future assessments to stave off bankruptcy, but this just kicked the can down the road. As losses mounted and the probability of a bailout declined, it became obvious the systems would fail.
The postwar price collapses and bank failures put pressure on the deposit insurance systems, and solvent banks abandoned them. Banks in both voluntary and involuntary deposit insurance systems could have avoided being subject to insurance and assessments by adopting a national bank charter. As shown in TableX, deposit insurance states saw a much higher number of national bank conversions than nearby states despite having about the same number of state banks during the period.26The ratio of converting banks to remaining state banks was significantly larger in deposit insurance states than in neighboring states before the collapse of the deposit insurance systems, and the difference grew almost eight times larger after the collapse began.27The ratio of the average capital in converted banks to the average capital in remaining state banks was also much larger in deposit insurance states than in the comparison states. Even in Kansas, where state banks could opt out of the system and retain their state charter, the rate of conversions to national banks and their relative size was larger than the averages for the comparison states. Because premium payments into the insurance systems were based on either the size of deposits or capital, the removal of these large and likely low-risk banks from the insured systems was a major blow to the prospects of the deposit insurance funds to raise future funds to cover losses.
Even before the crisis, large state banks in Kansas always had a greater tendency to avoid the insurance system. Based on the balance sheet data in 1908, just before deposit insurance was passed, Kansas state banks that opted out of deposit insurance from 1909 to 1920 had higher capital-to-asset ratios (22.95 versus 21.57%) and higher asset sizes ($142,464 versus $132,492) than those opting in.
Because of the large number of estimated coefficients, we display the results of equation6in Figure6.28The top panel presents the coefficients for deposits and the bottom panel contains the coefficients for loans. In each panel, we report the vector of β1coefficients, which is labeled “DI”; the vector of β2coefficients, which is labeled “State Bank*DIArea”; the vector of β3coefficients, which is labeled “DI*Insured”; as well as the vector of the total growth expected for insured banks after the installation of deposit insurance (i.e.,β1+β2+β3), which is labeled “Insured Banks Total.”
Outside of the initial large effect in 1908 and 1909, the effect of deposit insurance on deposits and loans is greatest during the WWI price increase. While uninsured bank deposits also rise over the WWI period, uninsured bank loans do not experience much of an increase. This is consistent with our findings that insured banks rode the WWI agricultural price bubble more than uninsured banks, especially for loans. After 1920, there was a massive decline in insured bank deposits and loans and a much less dramatic decline for uninsured banks. Indeed, the coefficients on uninsured banks largely stabilize by 1924, while the coefficients on insured banks continue to drop. Clearly, insured banks saw much larger gains in deposits and loans during the WWI bubble and much greater declines afterwards. Consistent with the findings of Dehejia and Lleras-Muney (2007), we therefore conclude that the deposit insurance systems did not cushion their banks during the post-WWI downturn, and may have led to significant negative consequences for economic growth.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Deposit insurance systems were associated with unusually high rates of expansion during the early 20thcentury, and especially during WWI. They were also associated with unusually high bank losses after WWI.
In this paper, we identify clearly the role of deposit insurance on depositor and banker behavior as we compare the behavior of insured and uninsured banks located in the same states as well as uninsured banks in neighboring states. We do so first through state-level aggregate comparisons, which track the overall changes in resources of the two systems within each state, and then through panel analyses of individual banks, where we focus on within-bank changes in behavior associated with the passage of deposit insurance.
First, we show that deposit insurance increased insured banks’ deposits and loans, and decreased their cash-to-assets ratios and capital-to-assets ratios. Second, deposit insurance increased risk by removing the market discipline in the deposit market that had been constraining erstwhile uninsured banks. Depositors apparently believed that insurance protection was credible and, therefore, were willing to place old and new deposits in insured banks despite their observably high-risk characteristics. Deposit insurance encouraged banks to increase their insolvency risk because doing so did not prevent them from competing aggressively for the deposits of uninsured banks operating nearby. In fact, increasing risk was necessary to fund the higher interest payments that presumably attracted depositors. Third, we find that voluntary insurance systems created weaker subsidies for risk-taking by insured banks than mandatory insurance systems.
The extent to which insured banks attracted deposits, and used those funds to expand their lending, depended on the risk opportunities available in their local economic environment. Cross-county variation in the appreciation of commodity production during the WWI agricultural price boom explains between one- and two-thirds of the observed effects of deposit insurance on deposit growth, loan growth, and increased risk-taking by insured banks. The fact that a large part of the moral hazard associated with deposit insurance depends on the time-varying and location-specific opportunities for risk-taking has important implications for empirical analysis of the consequences of deposit insurance in other contexts. The potential costs of deposit insurance may appear low in environments that are relatively lacking in risk-taking opportunities, but those costs can appear much higher when greater risk-taking opportunities present themselves. The absence of an apparent treatment effect during times of low risk-taking opportunities can explain why deposit insurance can be active for long periods of time without resulting in large-scale banking crises. For instance, after the establishment of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation in 1933, it was not until the real estate booms of the 1970s and early 1980s that the risk-taking opportunities were sufficient to produce significant moral hazard.
Insured banks suffered much higher failure rates at the end of WWI, and the collapse of these insurance systems resulted in losses to depositors in all but one of the states. The history of deposit insurance in the United States and internationally has been a process of increasing systemic risk in the name of reducing liquidity risk. The deeper lesson of that history is that economic models that attempt to explain the attraction of deposit insurance are less relevant than political ones, which prior studies have found very useful in explaining the enactment of deposit insurance (Demirgüç-Kunt, Kane and Laeven (2008), Calomiris (2010), Calomiris and Haber (2014), Calomiris and Jaremski (2016)).
Our historical sample of insured banks did not enjoy government guarantees and did not receive government bailouts when their funds collapsed. Given that the protection offered by deposit insurance in these states was weaker than under current systems of government-guaranteed insurance that operate today, our results provide a conservative measure of the risk-increasing incentives of deposit insurance: in the presence of government guarantees, insurance protection should be perceived as greater, and incentive distortions for risk-taking should be even larger.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
